dialogues: You've been describing arrays and building expressions and equations. Let's connect what you've been working on.
Check out these two pictures. A muffin tin and a dot array. What expression describes them both by rows?
Right. 2 times 4. Whether it's muffins or dots, 2 rows of 4 is 2 times 4. The arrangement is what matters, not what's in the spots.

Here's an array you've worked with before. If we count by rows, it's 3 times 5 equals 15. If we count by columns, it's 5 times 3 equals 15. Take a look at both equations. What do you notice about the products?
You got it. Same array, two different descriptions, but the product stays the same. Interesting, right? We'll explore why that works next time.
Arrays aren't just something we do in math class. Take a look at these four pictures. Which ones show arrays?
Yep. The egg carton, the parking lot, the window pane. Arrays are all around us. Same rows-and-columns structure you've been using.
Once you start seeing arrays, you'll spot them everywhere. Same math -- rows times columns -- whether it's eggs, cars, or windows.
If you had to pick one thing from today to remember, what would it be?
Rows and columns. That's what makes an array an array, not just scattered groups. You'll see that again.
Exactly. What you say is what you write. When your words are precise, your math is too.
Two ways to describe it, two expressions, same array. We'll build on that next time.

You came in knowing about equal groups in bags and boxes. Now you're seeing them lined up as arrays -- with rows and columns. You can describe the same array two different ways. You can write the expression for each one. And you can find the product.
You noticed something about those two products. Next time, we're going to figure out WHY that happens... and whether it works every time.
